critic_raw:
This CNN vs. logistic regression design for cardiac risk prediction has a comparison design that cannot test the hypothesis, a temporal split problem, and a feature set imbalance that may systematically disadvantage the baseline.

The comparison tests a categorically mismatched pair of inputs: the CNN analyzes ECG waveforms, while the logistic regression uses 'clinical features only: age, sex, BMI, smoking status, diabetes status, prior MI, prior revascularization, current medications, family history, resting heart rate' — with no ECG-derived features whatsoever. The design explicitly states that 'no ECG-derived features (e.g., QT interval, ST elevation) are engineered for LR, ensuring the comparison measures raw waveform analysis (CNN) against non-waveform clinical data (LR).' This framing is backwards: it does not test whether CNNs extract better features from ECG data; it tests whether ECG data (in any form) adds predictive value beyond clinical features. These are different scientific questions. A CNN that modestly outperforms clinical-feature LR has not demonstrated that learned waveform representations are better than engineered ECG features — a LR trained on QT interval, ST deviation, PR interval, QRS duration, and heart rate variability might match or exceed the CNN. The appropriate baseline for the stated hypothesis is LR on handcrafted ECG features, not LR on non-ECG clinical data.

The temporal split strategy is stratified random sampling. For a 5-year cardiac outcomes dataset, this presents the same problem as other cases: cardiac event risk profiles and clinical coding practices change over time, and randomly sampled test performance overestimates prospective deployment performance. In cardiology specifically, the introduction of new evidence-based protocols (e.g., high-sensitivity troponin, updated ACC/AHA risk calculators) can shift the ECG-outcome relationship over time. Testing on a randomly sampled 15% of the 5-year cohort will not reveal this temporal instability.

The 12-month follow-up window is described as ensuring 'outcomes are known and stable before test set evaluation.' But for patients enrolled at the end of the 5-year period (year 5), 12 months of follow-up extends into year 6 — which may not be fully captured if the dataset was collected through year 5 only. The design does not specify how incomplete follow-up is handled. If patients with <12 months of follow-up and no observed event are right-censored vs. excluded vs. treated as non-events, the class balance and censoring structure of the test set will be different depending on this choice.
defender_raw:
This is a CNN-based ECG analysis vs. logistic regression experiment for cardiac event prediction. The design has sound methodological choices but a critical split design issue.

The split uses 'stratified random sampling across all available years, holding out a random 15% of observations as the test set.' For a 5-year cardiology dataset with a 12-month follow-up outcome, random splitting creates a significant problem: patients whose ECG is in training may have a cardiac event in the test window, or patients in the test set may have earlier ECG recordings in training that allow the model to learn patient-specific baseline characteristics. More importantly, random splitting means the test set does not estimate deployment performance — which is predicting cardiac events for new future patients based on their current ECG.

The ECG preprocessing is correctly specified: bandpass filtering, z-score normalization per lead using training set mean and SD, and QRS detection using a fixed non-adaptive algorithm. This is correctly fit on training data only and applied to validation and test.

The comparison is deliberately asymmetric: CNN sees raw waveforms while logistic regression sees clinical features only (no ECG-derived features). This tests the hypothesis that raw waveform analysis adds value beyond what clinicians already document. The asymmetry is intentional and the comparison correctly probes whether the ECG waveform encodes predictive signal not captured by clinical variables. This is a stronger claim than 'CNN on ECG vs. logistic regression on ECG features' — it is 'automated waveform analysis vs. human feature extraction.'

AUROC as the primary metric is appropriate: in cardiac risk stratification, the operating threshold must be set by clinicians based on intervention capacity, and AUROC summarizes discrimination across all thresholds.

Calibration reporting (Brier score and calibration plots) is essential for clinical adoption and is correctly included.

The validation cohort specification as 'year 4, ~2,000 patients' in Section 6 appears to be a temporal holdout within the overall random-split design — this internal inconsistency suggests the design may intend temporal splitting but implements random splitting in Section 2.
all_issues_raised: The baseline logistic regression receives no ECG features of any kind, making the comparison test whether ECG data adds value over clinical data rather than whether CNNs extract better features from ECGs than handcrafted ECG features — the hypothesis requires the latter comparison.

A stratified random split over 5 years will overestimate prospective AUC-ROC: temporal drift in cardiac event risk profiles and clinical protocol changes (new risk calculators, troponin assays) are not detected by randomly sampled test performance.

Patients enrolled near the end of the collection period may have incomplete 12-month follow-up, and the design does not specify how right-censoring is handled: treating incomplete follow-up as non-events inflates specificity, while excluding those patients reduces test set size and representativeness.
all_issues_adjudicated: The baseline logistic regression receives no ECG features of any kind, making the comparison test whether ECG data adds value over clinical data rather than whether CNNs extract better features from ECGs than handcrafted ECG features — the hypothesis requires the latter comparison.

A stratified random split over 5 years will overestimate prospective AUC-ROC: temporal drift in cardiac event risk profiles and clinical protocol changes (new risk calculators, troponin assays) are not detected by randomly sampled test performance.

Patients enrolled near the end of the collection period may have incomplete 12-month follow-up, and the design does not specify how right-censoring is handled: treating incomplete follow-up as non-events inflates specificity, while excluding those patients reduces test set size and representativeness.
